md: Two important theorems with very different uses. The **Mean Value Theorem** is structural — it underlies many proofs in calculus. **L'Hôpital's Rule** is a practical tool — it solves indeterminate-form limits that algebraic methods can't crack.

blocks.title: Mean Value Theorem (MVT)
blocks.body: If $f$ is **continuous on $[a, b]$ and differentiable on $(a, b)$**, then there exists at least one $c$ in $(a, b)$ such that $f'(c) = \dfrac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}$.

md: **Plain English:** somewhere in the interval, the instantaneous slope (tangent) equals the average slope (secant) over the whole interval.
**Driving analogy:** if you average 60 mph over a trip, your speedometer must have read exactly 60 at some moment.
**Example.** $f(x) = x^2$ on $[1, 4]$. Average rate: $(16 - 1)/3 = 5$. MVT says some $c$ in $(1, 4)$ has $f'(c) = 5$. $f'(x) = 2x$, so $2c = 5 \Rightarrow c = 2.5$. Check: $2.5 \in (1, 4)$ ✓.

blocks.title: Rolle's Theorem
blocks.body: If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, differentiable on $(a, b)$, AND $f(a) = f(b)$, then there exists $c$ in $(a, b)$ with $f'(c) = 0$.

md: Same idea: if you start and end at the same height, you must have a 'flat moment' somewhere in between.
Useful for proving roots and counting zeros.
MVT is the foundation for many calculus theorems:

**Increasing/decreasing test:** if $f' > 0$ on an interval, then $f$ is increasing there. **Why?** For any $a < b$, MVT gives $f(b) - f(a) = f'(c)(b - a)$ for some $c$. If $f' > 0$, this is positive → $f(b) > f(a)$.
**Constant-derivative theorem:** if $f' = 0$ everywhere on an interval, $f$ is constant.
**Speed-from-distance bounds:** estimate average speed from instantaneous speed bounds.

blocks.title: L'Hôpital's Rule
blocks.body: If $\lim_{x \to a} f(x)/g(x)$ has indeterminate form $0/0$ or $\infty/\infty$, AND $f$ and $g$ are differentiable near $a$ with $g'(x) \neq 0$, then: $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} \dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \lim_{x \to a} \dfrac{f'(x)}{g'(x)}$ — provided the right-hand limit exists or is $\pm\infty$.

md: **The rule:** when you get $0/0$ (or $\infty/\infty$), differentiate the **numerator and denominator separately** (NOT using the quotient rule!) and try the limit again.
(This is the famous limit that powered the proof of $(\sin x)' = \cos x$.)
(Shows that $\ln x$ grows much slower than $x$.)

blocks.title: L'Hôpital warning
blocks.body: **Only use it on indeterminate forms.** If you mindlessly apply L'Hôpital to a non-indeterminate form, you'll get the wrong answer. **Always check that direct substitution gives 0/0 or ∞/∞ first.**

md: L'Hôpital handles $0/0$ and $\infty/\infty$ directly. Other indeterminate forms ($0 \cdot \infty$, $\infty - \infty$, $0^0$, $\infty^0$, $1^\infty$) need to be **rewritten** as $0/0$ or $\infty/\infty$ first.
**Example — $1^\infty$.** $\lim_{n \to \infty} (1 + 1/n)^n = e$ (this is the famous definition of $e$ from a calculus angle).
